text:
CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 55-year-old female with recently diagnosed nodular melanoma of the back, Breslow depth 6.1 mm, ulcerated, positive sentinel lymph nodes. Staging evaluation.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed following the administration of intravenous iohexol contrast material. Arterial and portal venous phase images obtained through the abdomen. Axial, coronal, and sagittal reformats reviewed. Comparison: No prior cross-sectional imaging available.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and airways: Multiple small bilateral pulmonary nodules are present, the majority measuring below 5 mm and not characterizable on this examination. The largest identified nodule is in the right lower lobe measuring approximately 6 mm in greatest dimension, with a rounded morphology. Additional nodules are scattered throughout both lower lobes. The airways are patent to the segmental level. No consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and hila: No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. No aortic abnormality.

Left axilla: Multiple enlarged left axillary lymph nodes are noted on the chest images, the largest measuring approximately 3.1 cm in short axis with loss of fatty hilum, rounded contour, and heterogeneous enhancement — findings highly suspicious for nodal involvement in this clinical setting.

Chest wall and soft tissues: No chest wall mass or rib destruction.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is enlarged. At least 8 discrete hepatic lesions are distributed throughout both lobes. The lesions are predominantly hypoenhancing on portal venous phase images. The largest lesion is in the right lobe measuring approximately 3.7 cm in greatest dimension, with irregular margins and central hypoattenuation suggesting internal necrosis or necrotic change. Additional lesions range from approximately 0.8 to 2.5 cm. The constellation of findings — number, distribution, enhancement characteristics, and clinical context — is consistent with metastatic involvement. No biliary ductal dilatation. Portal vein is patent.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal lesion.

Pancreas: Unremarkable. No ductal dilatation.

Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands appear within normal limits. No discrete adrenal mass.

Kidneys: The kidneys enhance symmetrically. No hydronephrosis or renal mass.

Bowel: No obstruction. No pneumoperitoneum.

Retroperitoneum: No convincingly enlarged retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymph nodes by size criteria.

---

PELVIS:

Bladder: Unremarkable.

Uterus/Ovaries: Uterus and ovaries are visualized without discrete mass lesion.

Iliac and inguinal lymph nodes: Bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy is present. The most prominent node is in the right inguinal chain, measuring 2.4 cm in short axis with an oval, minimally lobulated contour and absent fatty hilum. Additional bilateral inguinal nodes measure up to 1.6 cm on the left. Findings are concerning for nodal metastases.

---

SOFT TISSUES / SUBCUTANEOUS:

At least four subcutaneous nodules are identified along the trunk. The largest is along the right flank measuring approximately 1.8 cm, round, well-marginated, and demonstrating mild enhancement. Additional smaller nodules are scattered over the trunk. These are suspicious for subcutaneous metastatic deposits in the context of known melanoma.

SKELETAL STRUCTURES: No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesion identified. No pathologic fracture.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Multifocal hepatic lesions throughout both lobes, up to 3.7 cm, consistent with hepatic metastases in the setting of known melanoma. At least 8 lesions are identified; additional sub-centimeter lesions may not be fully resolved on this examination.

2. Bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy, most prominent on the right (2.4 cm short axis), compatible with nodal metastatic disease.

3. Left axillary lymphadenopathy, up to 3.1 cm, similarly suspicious for nodal involvement.

4. Multiple subcutaneous nodules along the trunk, largest 1.8 cm on the right flank, concerning for subcutaneous metastases.

5. Multiple small bilateral pulmonary nodules, the largest 6 mm in the right lower lobe. These are indeterminate; primary melanoma metastases cannot be excluded, though some may represent benign granulomatous disease given the patient's southwestern US residence. Recommend correlation with clinical findings.

6. No pleural effusion, pericardial effusion, or osseous metastasis.

Note: MRI brain with and without contrast obtained concurrently for intracranial staging — see separate report.

Electronic signature: [Attending Radiologist]
Dictated and finalized.
